skill.name: anndata
skill.description: Data structure for annotated matrices in single-cell analysis; use when reading/writing .h5ad (or zarr) and exchanging data with the scverse ecosystem.
skill.summary: Read, write, and manipulate single-cell genomics data using the AnnData library for Python.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 419 static findings are false positives. The skill contains only documentation and reference guides (SKILL.md + 5 markdown reference files). Weak cryptographic algorithm flags are caused by variable names in code examples. Ruby/shell backtick execution flags are Markdown code fence backticks in documentation blocks. No executable code, credential access, or malicious behavior was identified. The skill is safe to publish with a LOW risk level.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences mistaken for shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 379 instances of backtick characters in markdown files as Ruby/shell command execution. All backticks are Markdown code fence delimiters enclosing Python code examples. This is a false positive caused by the static analyzer interpreting Markdown syntax as shell injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backtick occurrences are in Markdown code fenced blocks containing Python documentation examples, not shell commands.

low_findings.title: Variable names flagged as weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged variable names like 'uns', 'result', and data attribute patterns as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are AnnData object attribute names and standard Python variable names in documentation code examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines contain only variable names, dictionary keys, or arithmetic operations in documentation code examples, not cryptographic algorithm implementations.

low_findings.title: Dictionary keys() method flagged as certificate/key files
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged Python .keys() dictionary method call on line 211 of concatenation.md as certificate or key file content. This is a standard Python dictionary method, not cryptographic key material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is `print(adata_combined.layers.keys())` which is a Python dict.keys() call in documentation.

low_findings.title: String label flagged as C2 keyword
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged the string 'Desc2' in a Python code example as a C2 (Command and Control) keyword. This is a short label for a data description column.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text 'Desc2' is a Python list element `['Desc2'] * 50` in an AnnData concatenation documentation example.

low_findings.title: Comments and data field names flagged as system/network reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged several comments and data field names in documentation as system or network reconnaissance. These are descriptive text about AnnData functionality, not actual reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines are documentation comments about AnnData data structures, file formats, or audit result notes, not active reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Example URLs and standard library imports flagged as network activity
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged use of example.com URLs for documentation purposes and standard Python urllib imports as network threats. These are legitimate documentation patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URL uses example.com (safe testing domain) and github.com (source attribution). urllib usage is documented example code for downloading genomics data files.

low_findings.title: Heuristic dangerous combination is a documentation false positive
low_findings.description: Static analyzer heuristic flagged a dangerous combination of code execution + network + credential access patterns, but this is triggered by documentation examples containing standard Python imports, file I/O, and example URLs. No actual credential access exists in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Combination of Python imports, file I/O, and example URLs in documentation triggered heuristic. No environment variable access, no credentials, no obfuscated code found in any file.

content.user_title: Analyze single-cell genomics data with AnnData
content.value_statement: Working with single-cell RNA-seq data requires managing complex annotated matrices. This skill helps you use the AnnData library to read, write, subset, concatenate, and manipulate single-cell datasets with Python.
actual_capabilities: Read and write AnnData objects in H5AD and Zarr formats
Subset and filter single-cell datasets while preserving annotation alignment
Concatenate multiple AnnData objects along observations or variables axis
Use backed mode for memory-efficient processing of large datasets
Access and modify metadata in obs, var, uns, and multi-dimensional slots
Convert between sparse and dense matrix representations for genomics data
limitations: Cannot perform statistical analysis like clustering or differential expression
Does not replace dedicated analysis tools like Scanpy for full workflows
Backed mode is read-only; data modifications require loading into memory
Requires familiarity with Python, Pandas, NumPy, and scientific computing

use_cases.title: Load and prepare single-cell datasets for analysis
use_cases.description: Read H5AD files from public datasets and inspect their structure including metadata, expression matrices, and embeddings.

use_cases.title: Merge multiple experiments from different batches
use_cases.description: Combine single-cell datasets from multiple experimental batches while tracking provenance with batch labels and merge strategies.

use_cases.title: Process large datasets that exceed available RAM
use_cases.description: Use backed mode to explore and subset large H5AD files without loading all data into memory at once.

prompt_templates.title: Load and inspect an H5AD file
prompt_templates.prompt: Load the H5AD file from 'path/to/data.h5ad' into an AnnData object. Print the shape, the list of observation columns in .obs, and the list of variable columns in .var. Check if the data matrix X is sparse or dense.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user opening an existing single-cell dataset for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Subset cells by metadata condition
prompt_templates.prompt: From the AnnData object, select only the cells where .obs['cell_type'] equals 'T cell'. Create a new AnnData object containing only these cells. Print the number of cells and genes in the subset.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user filtering cells by a categorical annotation

prompt_templates.title: Concatenate datasets with batch labels
prompt_templates.prompt: Concatenate two AnnData objects 'adata_a' and 'adata_b' along observations. Use inner join to keep only shared variables. Add a batch label column to .obs with keys 'condition_a' and 'condition_b'. Show the count of cells per batch.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user merging data from different experimental conditions

prompt_templates.title: Work with large data using backed mode
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the H5AD file in backed mode to read it without loading all data into memory. Extract the observation names where .obs['quality'] exceeds 0.9. Load only those cells into memory and print the shape of the resulting subset.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert user processing a dataset too large for available RAM

output_examples.input: Load file 'pbmc3k.h5ad' and summarize its structure.
output: AnnData object with n_obs x n_vars = 2638 x 1838
X matrix is sparse (csr_matrix) with 3.2 million non-zero entries

output_examples.input: Select all B cells and create a new AnnData object.
output: Annotations from the parent object preserved in .obs, .var, and .uns
The subset is a view by default. Use .copy() to create an independent object.

output_examples.input: Concatenate two datasets with batch labels.
output: Combined AnnData object: 4000 cells x 1500 genes
Inner join kept 1500 shared genes across both batches.

best_practices: Use sparse matrices (csr_matrix) for count-like single-cell data to reduce memory usage
Convert string annotations to categoricals with strings_to_categoricals() for efficient storage
Use backed='r' mode when exploring large H5AD files to avoid loading all data into memory
anti_patterns: Converting sparse matrices to dense with .toarray() or arithmetic operations that trigger densification
Modifying AnnData views instead of making explicit copies with .copy() before in-place changes
Writing H5AD files without compression, leading to unnecessarily large files on disk

faq.question: What is an AnnData object?
faq.answer: AnnData is a Python data structure for annotated matrices. It stores a data matrix X together with row annotations (obs), column annotations (var), unstructured metadata (uns), and multi-dimensional embeddings (obsm, varm). It is the standard data format in the scverse ecosystem.

faq.question: What is the difference between backed mode and in-memory mode?
faq.answer: In-memory mode loads the full data matrix into RAM. Backed mode (backed='r') keeps the data on disk and loads only requested slices. Use backed mode for large datasets that do not fit in memory.

faq.question: How do I combine multiple single-cell datasets?
faq.answer: Use ad.concat() to concatenate AnnData objects along observations (axis=0) or variables (axis=1). You can add batch labels with the label and keys parameters and control metadata handling with the merge parameter.

faq.question: What file formats does AnnData support?
faq.answer: AnnData natively supports H5AD (HDF5-based) and Zarr formats. It can also read CSV, Excel, Matrix Market (MTX), Loom, and 10X Genomics formats through converter functions.

faq.question: How does subsetting preserve alignment in AnnData?
faq.answer: When you subset an AnnData object with a boolean mask, the matrix rows are filtered and the corresponding obs and obsm entries are automatically aligned. Var and varm remain aligned to columns. This preserves the relationship between data and annotations.

faq.question: Can AnnData work with cloud storage like S3 or GCS?
faq.answer: Yes, AnnData can read Zarr stores from cloud storage using fsspec. The Zarr format is designed for cloud-friendly chunked access and supports parallel reading from remote storage systems.
